```
250-a=
foo
one

It's not one release per commit, it's one version per commit. Imo you should not have the same package version from two different commits, and you should never have to relay on somebody to make sure that's the case, this solves both. I don't think we want to change the name of the existing parameter.  If anyone currently uses `file_name` as a named argument, this will break their code.  It also breaks the config stuff that currently uses this parameter name (see `_req_params` below).

There are two options that I think would be ok.  First, you could add a new `hdu` parameter and have either `file_name` or `hdu` be required.  The new parameter should be put last to maintain backward compatibility for people who are relying on the current argument order.

Or second, you could leave the parameter name `file_name`, but just say that the file_name is allowed to be an HDU rather than a string, in which case that HDU is used rather than opening up the file.  This is probably the simpler option, and I think it would be fine.  Also, it would be almost identical to your existing code, just without the change to the parameter name.

```
tower-cli project get --organization=Default --tower-password=ASK

Then it looks up the related organization using the runtime settings. In the syntax I gave there, click processes the `--organization` option before the `--tower-password` option, which means that the organization will be looked up with the file settings, before the runtime settings have been applied. click supports only a single-tier of custom processing ordering, which is that eager params are processed first.

Yes, the compiled file depends not only on the files from the project, but also on everything else that is in the directory. For that reason, it should not be a responsibility of project build scripts (Makefiles) but one of package manager (as pacman does with post-install hooks).

Unfortunately, this falls short when one does not use a package manager (e.g. developer installing the app to `/usr/local/share` for testing), so most project do have `glib-compile-schemas` somewhere in their build scripts. 

One possible “solution” is building the caches (schemas, indexes, etc.) only if `DESTDIR` environment variable [is not set](https://github.com/GNOME/gnome-shell/blob/9f7b1014378a756f761908cc44da0ce07a35ab9d/meson/meson-postinstall.sh) and relying on the fact that most package managers set it.

To remove the issues with throwing an exception inside a generator, which breaks the generator, retry was moved INSIDE of YieldRowsData.read_rows.  When the iterator on ReadRowsResponses throws an exception, the iterator is recreated using the value of last_scanned_row_key.  See the new commit for details. 

There could be one final refactoring, to use Retry for the following:
```
response = six.next(self._response_iterator)
```
To do so would introduce a method on YieldRowsData, which would have access to the state information needed to recreate the iterator (self.request_pb and self.client).  However, this should be run by @jonparrott first, since his preference is to create a simple functools.partial, as was done for MutateRow.  In our case, since we need to modify the request_pb with the last_scanned_row_key, this extra state information needs to be conveyed. The saved client is also needed to create the new iterator. While this test method does potentially accomplish the goal of cancelling a task and seeing what happens, it could cause some collateral damage. What if we manage to implement parallelized tests? In that case, this would mess up other test cases. Far better to figure out a way to launch a long-running task from within this test method and cancel it. Or, if that can't be done, it's probably best to not perform this test.

We definitely want to do similar tests for both yum_importer and puppet_importer. 

In both cases we want to have tests that are using a feed. The sync task needs to be monitored via API and assertions need to be made about the task status and error field. These tests need to assert that a publish task was also spawned after the sync finishes. The publish task should also be monitored and the same assertions should be made about its status and errors. Then a few pieces of the published content should be retrieved via HTTP request from the published repository to make sure that it is actually published. 

We also need tests that check how uploading functionality works for RPMs and Puppet modules. This workflow is documented here [0]. All upload tests should include an API call to publish the newly uploaded content. Then the content should be retrieved via HTTP request to assert that it got published. 

[0] http://pulp.readthedocs.org/en/latest/dev-guide/integration/rest-api/content/upload.html

ha, this one was a tricky bug. But there's a possible sync problem:

if nodes A and B are running asynchronously, following can happen
1) A opens a channel
2) B also wants to open a channel at the same time, but A's transaction is already mined -> `self.api.channel_open()` raises `DuplicatedChannelError` (transaction throws)
https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/c72407fd6c7d1f6e01dc703a1ba6278091038ca2/raiden/connection_manager.py#L298
3) BUT B's state isn't yet synced to a block in which the A's open channel transaction is mined
4) if B proceeds without waiting, and calls `self.api.set_total_channel_deposit()`, an exception will be raised from here
https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/c72407fd6c7d1f6e01dc703a1ba6278091038ca2/raiden/api/python.py#L290
even if the channel exists on-chain (but not in the raiden state)

well this might work here,

but since i use a template for plugins,
it would make more work for me if i had to do this always,
when there is more than just `url` from the regex

it's easier to copy and paste with less changes 🦅 

```py
'''
source: -

Plugin for
- https://example.com
'''
import re

from streamlink.plugin import Plugin
from streamlink.plugin.api import http
from streamlink.stream import HLSStream

_url_re = re.compile(r'''https?://(?:\w+\.)?example\.com''')
_playlist_re = re.compile(r'''{\s+file:\s["'](?P<url>[^"']+)["']''')


class PLUGINNAME(Plugin):
    @classmethod
    def can_handle_url(cls, url):
        return _url_re.match(url)

    def _get_streams(self):
        res = http.get(self.url)

        match = _playlist_re.search(res.text)
        if match is None:
            return

        url = match.group('url')
        if url.startswith('//'):
            url = 'https:{0}'.format(url)

        if url:
            self.logger.debug('HLS URL: {0}'.format(url))
            yield 'live', HLSStream(self.session, url)

__plugin__ = PLUGINNAME

```
